originalText: कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन। मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि॥
transliteration: karmaṇy-evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana | mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stv akarmaṇi ||
translation: You have a right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of those actions. Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.
commentary: This is the central teaching of Karma Yoga. Krishna instructs Arjuna that action performed without attachment to results is the path of spiritual freedom. The ego binds us when we act for personal gain or avoid action from fear of failure.
practicalNote: When facing career confusion, decision anxiety, or fear of failure — act with full sincerity, release the outcome. Your duty is the action; the result belongs to dharma. This breaks the paralysis of outcome-dependence.

originalText: न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन् नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः। अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे॥
transliteration: na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||
translation: The soul is never born nor dies at any time. It has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. It is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and primeval. It is not slain when the body is slain.
commentary: Krishna teaches Arjuna that grief for the dead is misplaced because the soul (Atman) is eternal. The body perishes; the consciousness within it does not. Understanding this removes the root of existential fear and grief.
practicalNote: For those grieving loss or fearing death — this is the grounding teaching. What is real cannot be destroyed. What has passed is only the outer form. The essence remains.

originalText: तस्मादसक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर। असक्तो ह्याचरन् कर्म परमाप्नोति पूरुषः॥
transliteration: tasmād asaktaḥ satataṃ kāryaṃ karma samācara | asakto hy ācaran karma param āpnoti pūruṣaḥ ||
translation: Therefore, without attachment, perform always the work that has to be done, for by performing action without attachment, one attains the Supreme.
commentary: Action done without craving for personal benefit purifies the heart. The wise do what must be done — for the world, for others, for dharma — without making the self the center of the act.
practicalNote: In service work, leadership, or when burned out by thankless effort — this verse reframes the work itself as the offering. Let the action be the gift. Release the need for recognition.

originalText: उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत्। आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः॥
transliteration: uddhared ātmanātmānaṃ nātmānam avasādayet | ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ ||
translation: Let a person lift themselves up by their own mind, and not let the self be degraded. The mind alone is the friend of the conditioned soul, and the mind alone is the enemy of the conditioned soul.
commentary: The mind is the source of both liberation and bondage. When the mind is trained and still, it becomes the greatest ally. When it is restless and undisciplined, it becomes the primary obstacle. The work of self-improvement begins within.
practicalNote: For those struggling with self-criticism, depression, or self-sabotage — this verse places both the problem and the solution within you. Discipline of mind is the first discipline. Begin there.

originalText: अनन्याश्चिन्तयन्तो मां ये जनाः पर्युपासते। तेषां नित्याभियुक्तानां योगक्षेमं वहाम्यहम्॥
transliteration: ananyāś cintayanto māṃ ye janāḥ paryupāsate | teṣāṃ nityābhiyuktānāṃ yoga-kṣemaṃ vahāmy aham ||
translation: For those who worship Me with devotion, meditating on My transcendental form, I carry what they lack and preserve what they have.
commentary: Krishna's promise to the devoted: those who surrender to the divine with single-pointed focus need not carry anxiety about provision and protection. The devotional path is also a path of trust.
practicalNote: For those feeling unsupported, abandoned, or overwhelmed by lack — this is the devotional assurance. Surrender the worry. Act in devotion. What is needed will come.

originalText: सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज। अहं त्वां सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः॥
transliteration: sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja | ahaṃ tvāṃ sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ ||
translation: Abandon all varieties of religion and just surrender unto Me. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions. Do not fear.
commentary: This is the final and most intimate teaching of the Gita — the charama shloka. All systems, duties, paths and rules must ultimately lead to surrender. This is not abandonment of dharma but its fulfillment through complete trust.
practicalNote: For moments of complete overwhelm, moral confusion, or exhaustion from trying — this verse offers the path of surrender. Not weakness. Ultimate trust. 'Do not fear' is the closing message of the Gita.

transliteration: mātrā-sparśās tu kaunteya śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ | āgamāpāyino 'nityās tāṃs titikṣasva bhārata ||
translation: O son of Kunti, the non-permanent appearance of happiness and distress, and their disappearance in due course, are like the appearance and disappearance of winter and summer seasons. They arise from sense perception, and one must learn to tolerate them without being disturbed.
commentary: All pleasure and pain are temporary. They come and go like seasons. The practice is not to eliminate experience, but to develop steadiness in the face of it — equanimity regardless of condition.
practicalNote: For those in suffering who cannot see the end — this is the ground teaching. The season will pass. What matters is the steadiness of the one who observes it. Practice titiksha — patient endurance.

originalText: यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत। अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्॥ परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम्। धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे॥
transliteration: yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata | abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṃ sṛjāmy aham || paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṃ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām | dharma-saṃsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge ||
translation: Whenever there is a decline in righteousness and an increase in unrighteousness, O Arjuna, at that time I manifest myself. To deliver the pious and to annihilate the miscreants, as well as to reestablish the principles of dharma, I advent myself millennium after millennium.
commentary: The divine enters the world when dharma is most threatened. This teaching assures the devotee that righteousness is not permanently defeated — the movement of history bends toward dharma.
practicalNote: For those who feel that justice is impossible, that good is losing, or that the world is beyond repair — this verse is the cosmic assurance. Dharma is not fragile. It is self-restoring.

originalText: क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते। क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप॥
transliteration: klaibyaṃ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayy upapadyate | kṣudraṃ hṛdaya-daurbalyaṃ tyaktvottiṣṭha paraṃtapa ||
translation: Do not yield to impotence, O Arjuna. It does not befit you. Shake off your faint-heartedness and arise.
commentary: This is a direct, energizing call to courage. Krishna addresses Arjuna's collapse not with sympathy but with a firm call to stand. Sometimes compassion is the refusal to let someone remain small.
practicalNote: For moments of paralysis, cowardice, or collapse into helplessness — this verse is a sharp, loving command. Arise. The paralysis does not befit your nature. This is the voice of courage speaking.

originalText: अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च। निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी॥ सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः। मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः॥
transliteration: adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānāṃ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca | nirmamo nirahaṃkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣamī || santuṣṭaḥ satataṃ yogī yatātmā dṛḍha-niścayaḥ | mayy arpita-mano-buddhir yo mad-bhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ ||
translation: One who is not envious but is a kind friend to all living entities, who does not think himself a proprietor and is free from false ego, who is equal in both happiness and distress, who is forgiving, always satisfied, self-controlled, and engaged in devotional service with determination, his mind and intelligence fixed on Me — such a devotee of Mine is very dear to Me.
commentary: Krishna describes the qualities of the ideal devotee — free from hatred, ego, and possessiveness; steady in joy and sorrow. This is the portrait of a spiritually mature person in daily life.
practicalNote: When seeking to understand what true spiritual maturity looks like in practice — this is it. Not renunciation or performance of rituals, but the quality of being present with all people and conditions.

originalText: श्रीभगवानुवाच। असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम्। अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते॥
transliteration: asaṃśayaṃ mahābāho mano durnigrahaṃ calam | abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate ||
translation: O mighty-armed one, undoubtedly the mind is difficult to control and is restless. But it can be controlled by practice and detachment.
commentary: Krishna does not deny that the mind is hard to tame. He acknowledges it fully — then offers the two-part remedy: abhyasa (repetitive practice) and vairagya (non-attachment). Both are required.
practicalNote: For those struggling to meditate, build habits, or maintain focus — this verse names the reality: yes, the mind is hard to control. But practice and non-attachment are the tools. Begin the practice, even imperfectly.

transliteration: amānitvam adambhitvam ahiṃsā kṣāntir ārjavam | ācāryopāsanaṃ śaucaṃ sthairyam ātma-vinigrahaḥ ||
translation: Humility, freedom from pride, non-violence, patience, simplicity, approaching a genuine teacher, cleanliness, steadiness, and self-control — these are declared to be knowledge.
commentary: Krishna lists the qualities that constitute true wisdom — not intellectual accumulation but character formation. Real jnana is expressed in how one lives: with humility, patience, non-harmfulness.
practicalNote: For those seeking to understand what 'becoming wiser' actually means — this is the definition. Wisdom is not learning more facts. It is living with humility, patience, and non-violence.

originalText: सर्वभूतेषु येनैकं भावमव्ययमीक्षते। अविभक्तं विभक्तेषु तज्ज्ञानं विद्धि सात्त्विकम्॥
transliteration: sarva-bhūteṣu yenaikaṃ bhāvam avyayam īkṣate | avibhaktaṃ vibhakteṣu taj jñānaṃ viddhi sāttvikam ||
translation: That knowledge by which one sees a single imperishable reality in all beings — undivided in the divided — know that knowledge to be in the mode of goodness.
commentary: The highest form of knowledge is the perception of unity in diversity — seeing the same divine essence in all living beings. This perception ends separation, hatred, and the sense of other.
practicalNote: For those experiencing isolation, conflict with others, or a sense of separation — this teaching points to the root resolution. When you see yourself in others, the conflict changes its nature.

originalText: ध्यायतो विषयान् पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते। सङ्गात् संजायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते॥ क्रोधाद्भवति संमोहः संमोहात् स्मृतिविभ्रमः। स्मृतिभ्रंशाद् बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात् प्रणश्यति॥
transliteration: dhyāyato viṣayān puṃsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate | saṅgāt saṃjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate || krodhād bhavati saṃmohaḥ saṃmohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ | smṛti-bhraṃśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati ||
translation: While contemplating on objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them. From attachment springs desire, and from desire springs anger. From anger comes delusion; from delusion, confused memory; from confused memory, loss of intelligence; and from loss of intelligence one falls down again into the material pool.
commentary: Krishna maps the cascade from sense indulgence to total loss of wisdom. This is not moralism — it is psychology. Attention fed to an object becomes attachment. Attachment becomes craving. Craving becomes rage when thwarted.
practicalNote: For those struggling with addictive behaviors, anger issues, or escalating conflict — this verse traces the chain precisely. Interrupt the chain at attention, before it becomes attachment.

originalText: अहमात्मा गुडाकेश सर्वभूताशयस्थितः। अहमादिश्च मध्यं च भूतानामन्त एव च॥
transliteration: aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarva-bhūtāśaya-sthitaḥ | aham ādiś ca madhyaṃ ca bhūtānām anta eva ca ||
translation: I am the Self, O Gudakesha, seated in the hearts of all creatures. I am the beginning, the middle, and the end of all beings.
commentary: The divine is not separate from creation or from you. It is the witness seated within every heart. This is the mystical heart of Advaita — the divine is not other than what you already are.
practicalNote: For those seeking direct spiritual experience beyond ritual or belief — this verse points inward. You need not search far. The divine is the witness within you. Turn attention there.

originalText: प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः। अहङ्कारविमूढात्मा कर्ताहमिति मन्यते॥
transliteration: prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ | ahaṃkāra-vimūḍhātmā kartāham iti manyate ||
translation: All actions are carried out by the gunas (qualities) of nature. One whose mind is deluded by ego thinks: 'I am the doer.'
commentary: The ego's claim to authorship of actions is itself a delusion produced by nature (prakriti). True seeing dissolves the 'I am doing this' claim. This does not mean passivity — it means acting without ego inflation.
practicalNote: For those inflated by success or crushed by failure — both are reactions of the ego claiming authorship. The teaching: you are the instrument. Act fully. But the ego that claims 'I did this' is the delusion.

originalText: भोक्तारं यज्ञतपसां सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम्। सुहृदं सर्वभूतानां ज्ञात्वा मां शान्तिमृच्छति॥
transliteration: bhoktāraṃ yajña-tapasāṃ sarva-loka-maheśvaram | suhṛdaṃ sarva-bhūtānāṃ jñātvā māṃ śāntim ṛcchati ||
translation: The wise, knowing Me as the enjoyer of all sacrifices and austerities, the supreme Lord of all worlds, and the beneficent friend of all beings, attains peace.
commentary: Peace comes not from achieving desired outcomes, but from knowing the nature of the divine: benevolent, universal, the ultimate recipient of all sincere offering. Nothing you do goes unseen or unwelcomed.
practicalNote: For those seeking peace — not after achieving something, but as a practice — this verse offers the ground: know that the divine is a friend to all beings. You are not alone. Your offerings are received.

originalText: रसोऽहमप्सु कौन्तेय प्रभास्मि शशिसूर्ययोः। प्रणवः सर्ववेदेषु शब्दः खे पौरुषं नृषु॥
transliteration: raso 'ham apsu kaunteya prabhāsmi śaśi-sūryayoḥ | praṇavaḥ sarva-vedeṣu śabdaḥ khe pauruṣaṃ nṛṣu ||
translation: I am the taste of water, O Arjuna. I am the light of the sun and the moon. I am the syllable Om in the Vedic mantras; I am the sound in ether and ability in humans.
commentary: The divine is not located elsewhere — it is the very quality of experience itself. The taste of water. The light of the moon. The sacred syllable OM. The capacity in human beings to create and act.
practicalNote: For those seeking to experience the sacred in daily life — this verse is the guide. Taste water consciously. Notice light. Hear the sound of silence. The divine is not absent from the world — it is the world's essence.
answerUseCases: finding the sacred in daily life
